title: Average Precision (AP)
concept: Average Precision (AP) is the area under the Precision-Recall curve for a single object class, summarizing its performance.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What curve shape represents the best detector performance?

options.text: A curve that hugs the top-right corner of the plot
A straight diagonal line from bottom-left to top-right
A flat horizontal line at y=0

exercises.explain: A curve hugging the top-right maximizes both precision and recall.

cards.title: Summarizing the Curve
cards.body: If a student takes 10 quizzes throughout the semester, you don't show the parents all 10 quiz papers every time. You calculate a single average grade (like 85%) to summarize their performance. Average Precision is that summary grade for a single object class.

cards.title: Average Precision (AP)
cards.body: Average Precision (AP) is a metric that summarizes the Precision-Recall curve into a single value between 0.0 and 1.0. It is mathematically calculated as the **Area Under the Curve (AUC)**, usually using an interpolated average of precision values at different recall points.

cards.title: Area of the Field
cards.body: Imagine your Precision-Recall curve is the fence around a park. The total land area inside the fence (the Area Under the Curve) represents the quality of the park. A larger area means you have a better park (higher overall precision and recall)!

cards.title: Calculating class AP
cards.body: To find AP for the class 'Car', we calculate the precision at 11 standard recall points (0.0, 0.1, ..., 1.0) on the curve and average them. If the average is 0.78, then the AP for Car is 0.78.

cards.title: AP is not the overall score
cards.body: It's a myth that AP is the score for the entire model. AP is calculated for **one specific class at a time**. A model will have one AP score for cars, a different AP score for dogs, and a different one for traffic lights.

cards.title: Area Under the Curve
cards.body: Visualizing the geometric meaning of Average Precision:

left.text:
Curve hugs the top-right.
Shaded area covers most of the graph.

right.text:
Curve drops quickly.
Shaded area is very small.

exercises.prompt: What does Average Precision (AP) measure geometrically?
exercises.explain: AP corresponds to the Area Under the Curve (AUC) of the Precision-Recall plot for a given class.

options.text: The Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve for a single class
The height of the classification loss curve
The width of the anchor box grid
The total number of pixels in the bounding box

exercises.prompt: True or False: A model has a single AP score that represents its accuracy across all object categories simultaneously.
exercises.explain: False. AP is calculated independently for each individual class (e.g., one AP for 'cat', one for 'bicycle').
Average Precision is derived directly from the Precision-Recall curve.
exercises.sentence: AP is calculated as the Area Under the ___ curve.

exercises.prompt: Which value represents a possible Average Precision (AP) score?
exercises.explain: AP represents an area ratio and must be a fraction between 0.0 and 1.0.

exercises.prompt: You train a detector. The AP for the class 'dog' is 0.95, and the AP for the class 'person' is 0.40. What does this tell you about the model's performance?

options.text: The model is excellent at detecting dogs, but struggles significantly to detect people.
The model detects people faster than it detects dogs.
The class 'person' has more anchor boxes.
The training loss for dogs was higher.

exercises.explain: Exactly! The high AP for 'dog' means the Precision-Recall curve for dogs is near-perfect, while the low AP for 'person' indicates many missed detections or false alarms. Next: let's learn how we average these class scores into a final grade!
